content.problemStatement:
You have initially a string of **N** characters, denoted by **A1,A2...AN**. You have to print the size of the largest subsequence of string **A** such that all the characters in that subsequence are distinct ie. no two characters in that subsequence should be same.

A subsequence of string **A** is a sequence that can be derived from **A** by deleting some elements  and without changing the order of the remaining elements.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string S consisting of printable ASCII characters (code 0‑255). The length of S is N (1 ≤ N ≤ 100000).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the maximum possible length of a subsequence of S that contains only distinct characters.

sampleTestCases.explanation: All three characters are different, so the whole string can be taken as the subsequence.
The character 'a' appears twice; we can keep one 'a' and the 'b', giving a subsequence of length 2.
All characters are distinct, therefore the answer equals the string length.

hidden.label: tricky case with spaces and punctuation

solution.approach: The longest subsequence with all distinct characters can contain at most one occurrence of each character present in the original string. Hence the answer equals the number of different characters that appear in the string. We simply scan the string once, mark each character (0‑255) as seen using a boolean array of size 256, and count how many new characters are encountered. This works because the order of characters does not matter for a subsequence – we can always pick the first occurrence of each distinct character. The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and uses O(1) extra memory (256 booleans).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total length of all strings) overall
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – a fixed array of 256 booleans
